behaviors.description: Products marked as out-of-stock are excluded from recommendation results regardless of relevance score
behaviors.justification: The availability check is pure filtering logic. Real failure is algorithmic (missing the availability flag check or incorrect conditional). Unit test with mock catalog (mixed availability statuses) catches this directly. No I/O or infrastructure dependency; mocks reliably detect a missing or broken filter.
behaviors.test_description: Create mock product catalog with products having availability=true and availability=false. Call recommendation function with a user. Verify all returned products have availability=true. Test with high-scoring products that are out-of-stock to confirm they're excluded despite relevance.

behaviors.description: User preference filter correctly excludes product categories the user has explicitly blocked
behaviors.justification: Pure filtering logic applied to user settings. Real failure is missing the filter check or incorrect comparison. Unit test with mock user settings and product catalog can verify the filter is applied correctly. No state, persistence, or infrastructure dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Create user with known blocked_categories list. Create mock product catalog with products in both blocked and non-blocked categories. Call recommendation function. Assert that no returned product has a category in the user's blocked list.

behaviors.description: Recommendation algorithm ranks products matching recent browsing patterns higher than products with no browsing signal
behaviors.justification: The ranking logic is deterministic. Real failure is incorrect weighting of browsing signal (wrong coefficient, wrong recency decay, or missing signal entirely). Unit test with mock browsing history and product catalog can verify ranking order. No external dependency that mocks would hide.
behaviors.test_description: Create user with known recent browsing history (e.g., viewed 3 shoes this hour). Create product set with: products matching browsed categories, products in unbrowsed categories. Call recommendation function. Verify that products in browsed categories appear earlier in ranked list than unbrowsed ones.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns correct page with stable ordering — requesting page 2 twice returns identical results if data unchanged
behaviors.justification: Stability of ordering is an infrastructure property. Unit tests with mocks can verify pagination logic (offset/limit), but real failures in ordering determinism depend on: floating-point sorting comparison, database query result ordering, hash-based data structure iteration, or caching layers. These require real/realistic data structures and infrastructure behavior to detect.
behaviors.test_description: Insert realistic product set into test database. Request page 2 of recommendations twice in immediate succession. Collect ordered result lists. Deep-compare lists for exact equality (same products, same order, same scores). Verify bit-for-bit determinism even with floating-point scores.

behaviors.description: Algorithm surfaces at least one product from a category user hasn't browsed (serendipity requirement), avoiding filter bubble
behaviors.justification: Serendipity logic is algorithmic (e.g., reserve a slot, force-include top product from unbrowsed category). Real failure is missing implementation or incorrect category detection. Unit test with mock browsing history and product catalog can verify. No dependency that requires infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Create user with browsing history limited to 2 categories (e.g., shoes, socks). Create product catalog with products across 5+ categories. Call recommendation function (assuming serendipity is enabled). Verify at least one returned product is from a category NOT in user's browsing history.

behaviors.description: New user with no purchase or browsing history receives recommendations based on popularity, not empty result
behaviors.justification: Fallback logic is algorithmic. Real failure is missing fallback path or fallback returning empty/invalid results. Unit test with empty user history and mock popularity data can verify. No state or persistence dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Create new user with empty purchase_history and empty browsing_history. Mock popularity_scores for products. Call recommendation function. Assert that recommendations are non-empty and ranked by popularity score (highest first). Verify fallback path was triggered.

behaviors.description: Click tracking records correct product ID, position in list, and timestamp for each recommendation interaction
behaviors.justification: Click data must be persisted to real/test database. Unit mocks would hide real failures: data truncation in schema, timestamp generation bugs (wrong timezone, precision loss, system clock issues), schema mismatches, or incorrect field mapping on write. Real persistence layer is needed to catch these defects.
behaviors.test_description: In test environment with real/in-memory database: simulate user clicking recommendation at position 3 for product_id 'prod_123'. Record click timestamp. Query database directly. Assert that click record exists with: product_id='prod_123', position=3, timestamp matches within 1 second, all fields non-null and correct type.

behaviors.description: Response time for generating recommendations stays under 200ms for a catalog of 100,000 products
behaviors.justification: Performance is an empirical property that cannot be tested with small mock datasets. Real failure is algorithmic complexity (O(n²) instead of O(n log n)) or missing indexes in database queries. Requires realistic data volume (100k products) and actual algorithm execution to measure true latency.
behaviors.test_description: Load test database with exactly 100,000 products (realistic distribution across categories, availability, popularity scores). Create user with realistic browsing/purchase history. Execute recommendation request. Measure end-to-end latency from function entry to response completion. Assert latency < 200ms. Repeat 10 times to verify consistency.

behaviors.description: When a recommended product is purchased, algorithm deprioritizes it in subsequent requests
behaviors.justification: Requires accurate purchase history state and its retrieval by the algorithm. Unit mocks could hide failures in data layer: purchase not recorded, purchase history query broken, or incorrect field mapping. Real failure boundary is purchase persistence and retrieval, not just the ranking logic.
behaviors.test_description: Record that user purchased product_id 'prod_456'. Request recommendations immediately after. Verify 'prod_456' either absent or ranked significantly lower than before purchase (bottom 10% of results). Repeat request 3 times to confirm consistent deprioritization.

behaviors.description: Recommendation results are deterministic for same user state — requesting twice with no intervening activity returns identical ranked list
behaviors.justification: Determinism depends on infrastructure behavior: database query ordering, floating-point rounding, hash iteration order, or random seed initialization. Unit tests with mocks might pass if they don't expose non-determinism in actual system. Real failure requires infrastructure-level testing with actual data ordering and execution paths.
behaviors.test_description: Request recommendations for user twice in rapid succession (no user activity between requests). Collect both result lists with all fields (product_id, score, rank, timestamp). Deep-compare for exact equality: same products, same order, same scores. Repeat 20 times with different users to ensure consistent determinism.
